situation: Married couple, $20M-$60M, wants estate reduction but fears irrevocably giving money away
trusts: Spousal Lifetime Access Trust (SLAT), usually with dynasty provisions
whyItFits: Uses exemption now; the beneficiary spouse keeps household access; growth compounds outside both estates.
watchOuts: Divorce, or the beneficiary spouse dying first, cuts off indirect access. Consider life insurance on the beneficiary spouse and careful floating-spouse / trust-share drafting.

situation: Both spouses want their own SLATs
trusts: Two non-reciprocal Spousal Lifetime Access Trusts (SLATs)
whyItFits: Doubles the exemption used while each spouse still benefits from the other's trust.
watchOuts: Reciprocal trust doctrine: mirror-image trusts created together can be uncrossed and pulled back into both estates. Vary the terms, trustees, powers, assets, and timing.

situation: Estate is illiquid — an operating business or real estate dominates
whyItFits: Converts an annual premium into estate-tax-free liquidity available the day it is needed; no fire sale of the business.
watchOuts: Missed Crummey notices, retained incidents of ownership, or dying within 3 years of transferring an existing policy each drag the benefit back into the estate.

situation: Founder with volatile pre-IPO or concentrated public stock, wants to keep the exemption dry
trusts: Zeroed-out rolling Grantor Retained Annuity Trusts (GRATs)
whyItFits: Transfers only the excess over the Section 7520 hurdle; a flop costs almost nothing; no exemption consumed.
watchOuts: Rule one: don't die during the term. Also useless for grandchildren (no Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax (GST) allocation during the term — see the ETIP tab), and the math is tougher when the 7520 rate is high, as now.

situation: Founder or early employee holding Qualified Small Business Stock (QSBS) ahead of an exit
trusts: Irrevocable NON-grantor trusts — one per additional exclusion desired (see the QSBS Stacking Fit row on the Exec Summary)
whyItFits: Each non-grantor trust stacks its own Section 1202 cap on top of the founder's own exclusion.
watchOuts: Gift too close to a signed deal and the IRS can treat the sale as the founder's (assignment of income). A SLAT is a grantor trust and does NOT stack. California taxes the gain regardless — set state-tax expectations early.

situation: $30M+ with cash-flowing business, real estate, or fund interests
trusts: Seed gift plus installment sale to an Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust (IDGT) on a dynasty chassis
whyItFits: Freezes the estate at the note's Applicable Federal Rate (AFR); the grantor's income-tax payments quietly shift additional wealth every year.
watchOuts: Skimp on the ~10% seed capital or the appraisal and the whole sale is vulnerable. Dying with the note outstanding is expensive and legally unsettled — retire the note during life.

situation: Fund manager gifting General Partner (GP) or carried interest
trusts: Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust (IDGT) or Spousal Lifetime Access Trust (SLAT) holding a vertical slice of the GP interests
whyItFits: Carry's asymmetric upside is exactly the kind of asset you want compounding outside the estate from day one, at today's low value.
watchOuts: Section 2701: gift a proportionate vertical slice of the entire GP interest, or the deemed-gift rules can tax the transfer at far more than what was actually given.

trusts: Dynasty Trust sitused in South Dakota, Nevada, or Delaware
whyItFits: One Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax (GST) exemption allocation shields every future generation from the 40% toll.
watchOuts: California is a poor situs (rule against perpetuities, fiduciary income tax). Sloppy trustee and governance design turns a century-long vehicle into a century-long family fight — spend the money on drafting.

trusts: Qualified Personal Residence Trust (QPRT) — attractive at today's 5.2% Section 7520 rate
whyItFits: A high rate means a large retained-use discount on the gift; post-term fair-market rent keeps draining the estate tax-free.
watchOuts: Die during the term and the full value comes back. Heirs take carryover basis, and California Prop 19 usually means property-tax reassessment at term end — run all three numbers before committing.

situation: Charitably inclined, concentrated low-basis stock, wants retirement income
trusts: Charitable Remainder Unitrust (CRUT) plus a wealth-replacement Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust (ILIT)
whyItFits: Tax-free diversification, lifetime income, and an upfront deduction that grows with the Section 7520 rate; the insurance makes the heirs whole.
watchOuts: Do not fund with S-corporation stock or mortgaged property. A binding sale agreement before funding equals assignment of income. Early death favors charity over family — which is what the ILIT is for.

situation: Strong charitable intent plus one unusually high-income year (business sale, large bonus)
whyItFits: A large upfront charitable deduction lands against the spike year; the remainder passes to the children at a discounted gift value.
watchOuts: The grantor keeps paying tax on trust income for the whole term with no further deduction. Death mid-term recaptures part of the deduction. A high Section 7520 rate raises the hurdle — time the funding month.

situation: Second marriage or blended family — support the spouse, protect the children's remainder
trusts: Qualified Terminable Interest Property (QTIP) Trust with an independent trustee; often paired with a Credit Shelter Trust (both on the Situational Trusts tab)
whyItFits: The QTIP secures the marital deduction and a second basis step-up while locking the remainder for the children; the bypass share captures exemption at the first death.
watchOuts: The income spouse and the remainder children have opposed incentives — a family-member trustee inflames it. Missed QTIP elections are unfixable. New York residents: mind the state-only QTIP election.

situation: Surviving spouse is not a U.S. citizen
trusts: Qualified Domestic Trust (QDOT) — or naturalization before the estate tax return is filed (Situational Trusts tab)
whyItFits: The only path to the marital deduction for a non-citizen spouse; lifetime gifts to that spouse are capped at $194,000/year (2026).
watchOuts: Estate tax is withheld on principal distributions — deferral, not forgiveness. Sometimes the citizenship application is the cheapest estate planning available.

situation: Single client — no spouse to anchor a SLAT
trusts: Grantor Retained Annuity Trusts (GRATs), Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust (IDGT) sales, dynasty gifts; a Domestic Asset Protection Trust (DAPT) only if self-settled access is essential (Situational Trusts tab)
whyItFits: The same freeze-and-shift toolkit works; access simply cannot come through a spouse.
watchOuts: Self-settled access is the price of being single here — DAPT case law is thin and California courts do not respect it for California residents. Do not build the plan on the assumption of getting the money back.

situation: Client is under ~$15M single / ~$30M married, or elderly with low-basis assets
trusts: Usually none of these — revocable trust, portability election, and holding assets for the basis step-up
whyItFits: No federal estate tax will be due, so the free basis step-up at death is the single most valuable planning asset available.
watchOuts: The classic error: irrevocably gifting low-basis assets to dodge a phantom estate tax and handing the heirs a very real capital-gains bill instead. Don't pay 40%-style insurance premiums against a tax that never comes.

situation: California resident hoping to duck state income tax with an out-of-state trust
watchOuts: Since 2023, California taxes incomplete-gift non-grantor (ING) trusts as grantor trusts (New York killed them in 2014). Completed-gift non-grantor trusts can still work, but the exemption cost plus fiduciary and beneficiary residency rules must actually pencil.

situation: Client dreads decades of trustee and CPA overhead
trusts: Consolidate: fewer, larger, flexible trusts with decanting and trust-protector provisions
whyItFits: Every entity is a recurring cost and error surface for life; consolidation preserves optionality while cutting drag.
watchOuts: A trust that saves $100K of tax but costs $8K/yr of professional drag for 40 years was not a bargain. Model the full lifecycle cost — small special-purpose trusts (health, education, etc.) are the usual offenders.

situation: Grantor is getting tired of paying the trust's income-tax bill
trusts: Grantor-trust toggle-off power, or a carefully drafted discretionary tax-reimbursement clause
whyItFits: The burn can be turned off — if the document was drafted for it from the start.
watchOuts: A mandatory reimbursement right pulls the trust back into the estate, and even discretionary reimbursements used in a pre-arranged pattern invite the same result. Discretionary only, used sparingly.
